title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR introduces critical security vulnerabilities that must be fixed before merge: secrets committed to version control, PCI-DSS violations (storing and exporting card data), hardcoded backdoors in auth, eval-based code injection, and SSRF in webhooks. Additionally, several correctness and performance issues compound the risk. The team cannot approve this in its current state.

findings.title: Secrets committed to version control
findings.message: The .env file contains production secrets (JWT_SECRET, API keys) and is being committed to the repository. Once a secret hits git history it is compromised forever and must be rotated immediately. Secrets belong in a secret manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) injected at runtime, never in version control. Delete this file from history using `git filter-branch` or `bfg`, rotate all exposed secrets, and enforce pre-commit hooks to prevent recurrence.

findings.title: .gitignore modified to opt secrets into version control
findings.message: The change `!.env` explicitly opts the .env file (containing secrets) into source control, overriding the default ignore. This defeats the purpose of .gitignore and is a critical security anti-pattern. Revert this change and never commit .env files. Use .env.example with placeholder values for local development setup documentation.

findings.title: PCI-DSS violation: storing card number and CVC
findings.message: The transaction object is persisted with `card_number` and `cvc` fields. PCI-DSS forbids storing card verification values (CVC) anywhere, ever. Card numbers must be tokenized or encrypted—never stored in plaintext. The charge operation should return a `gatewayReference` only; the payment gateway handles card tokenization. Strip card details immediately after authorization; do not save them to the transaction.

findings.title: PCI-DSS violation: exporting card numbers in CSV
findings.message: The CSV export includes `card_number` in both header (line 48) and row data (line 55). This violates PCI-DSS. CSV exports must never contain card data. If transaction records are needed for support, tokenize or redact card numbers before export, or use a separate audit log that doesn't include plaintext card data.

findings.title: PCI-DSS violation: logging card data to console
findings.message: The line `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction));` logs the full transaction object, including card_number and CVC, to stdout. PCI-DSS prohibits plaintext card data in logs. Replace with `logger.info({ transactionId: transaction.id }, 'charge processed')` — the pino logger is configured with a redaction allowlist for sensitive fields. Never use `console.log` for transaction data.

findings.title: Insecure password hashing: MD5 is unsalted and instantly rainbow-tabled
findings.message: MD5 is cryptographically broken for password hashing — it is unsalted, fast, and the hash of common passwords is instantly available in public rainbow tables. Replace with bcrypt (with a work factor of at least 12) or Argon2. Example: `await bcrypt.hash(password, 12)` and `await bcrypt.compare(password, hash)`. Do not use createHash for passwords.

findings.title: Hardcoded admin master key backdoor in token verification
findings.message: The verifyToken function contains a literal hardcoded bypass: `if (token === 'admin-master-key') { return { userId: 'admin', ... } }`. This is a permanent backdoor that grants admin access to anyone who knows the string. Delete this check immediately. Authentication must never have a hardcoded bypass.

findings.title: Query parameter admin bypass allows anyone to become admin
findings.message: The line `if (req.query.adminBypass === '1') { req.user = { role: 'admin' } }` grants admin access to anyone who appends `?adminBypass=1` to any request. This is a permanent backdoor in production. Delete this bypass entirely. If you need a staging/debug mode, use a separate environment variable (`ALLOW_ADMIN_BYPASS`) checked at startup, never a request parameter.

findings.title: Email-domain admin check allows account spoofing
findings.message: Admin access is granted to anyone whose email ends with `@honeybook.com` or `@admin.com`. An attacker who controls an email account at honeybook.com (or registers honeybook.com.attacker.com) becomes admin. Email domain is not an identity — it is trivially spoofable in the request and cannot be trusted for authorization. Use a real identity mechanism: an explicit admin flag in a database, an identity provider (OAuth), or signed credentials.

findings.title: Duplicated weak email-domain admin checks in controller
findings.message: Each admin endpoint (listUsers, listTransactions, stats, exportCsv) manually repeats the check `if (!email.endsWith('@admin.com') && !email.endsWith('@honeybook.com') && req.query.adminBypass !== '1')`. This duplicates the same broken authorization logic 4 times, making it harder to fix. Centralize in the requireAdmin middleware (which is already imported but unused here). Also note: the adminBypass query check here makes the auth bypass persistent across all admin endpoints.

findings.title: Code injection via eval() on user-supplied WHERE clause
findings.message: The evalWhere function uses `eval(expr)` on a user-controlled `filter` parameter from the URL. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript: `email='a' || require('child_process').exec('rm -rf /') ||'`. This is remote code execution. Do not use eval. Instead, build a safe query object or use a library (e.g., mongo-query-language) that parses and validates filter expressions without executing user code.

findings.title: Code injection via new Function() on user-supplied filter
findings.message: The listAllTransactions method builds a function from user input: `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. An attacker can inject arbitrary code. Same as eval() — this is RCE. If filter expressions are needed, use a safe expression parser or whitelist allowed patterns. Never construct functions from untrusted input.

findings.title: SSRF vulnerability: fetching user-supplied URLs without validation
findings.message: The fireForTransaction method calls `await fetch(hook.url, { ..., redirect: 'follow' })` where hook.url is attacker-controlled (any registered webhook URL). An attacker can register `http://169.254.169.254/...` (AWS metadata), `http://localhost:8080/admin`, or `file://` to exfiltrate internal data. Mitigations: (1) validate protocol (https only), (2) resolve the hostname and block private/link-local ranges (127.0.0.1, 10.0.0.0/8, 169.254.x.x, ::1, etc.), (3) don't follow redirects, (4) enforce a timeout. Better: require webhook URLs to be HTTPS and pre-approve them.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET falls back to a known dev secret in production
findings.message: The line `const SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'dev-secret-123'` means that if the JWT_SECRET env var is missing or empty, the code uses a hardcoded, publicly-visible fallback secret. This is a path to forged tokens in production. Require the secret: `const SECRET = required('JWT_SECRET'); // throw if missing`. Never have a fallback secret; fail fast at startup if the environment is misconfigured.

findings.title: JWT signed without expiresIn or algorithm pinning
findings.message: The signToken call is `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` with no algorithm or expiry. Without expiresIn, tokens live forever (a leaked token is valid forever). Without algorithm pinning, a verifier that does not explicitly check `algorithms: ['HS256']` is vulnerable to algorithm-confusion attacks (e.g., `alg: 'none'`). Fix: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '15m' })` and verify with `jwt.verify(token, SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })`.

findings.title: JWT verified without pinning algorithm
findings.message: The verifyToken call does not pin the algorithm: `jwt.verify(token, SECRET)`. An attacker can send a token with `alg: 'none'` or use algorithm-confusion to bypass verification. Always explicitly set: `jwt.verify(token, SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })`.

findings.title: Predictable password reset tokens using Math.random() and Date.now()
findings.message: The generateResetToken function uses `Math.random() + Date.now()` to create reset tokens. Math.random() is not a CSPRNG and is not suitable for security tokens — the output space is small and predictable. An attacker can brute-force reset tokens. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` instead, which is cryptographically secure and has sufficient entropy.

findings.title: Password hash returned in response body
findings.message: The register endpoint returns `password_hash: user.password_hash` in the JSON response (line 30). The password hash is the crown jewel of the auth system — it should never be serialized or sent to the client. Remove it from the response; return only { id, email, role }.

findings.title: Login endpoint leaks whether email exists (email enumeration)
findings.message: The login endpoint returns `404` for "no user with that email" (line 44) and `401` for "wrong password" (line 49). This allows an attacker to enumerate valid email addresses by observing the status code. Return a uniform `401 Unauthorized` for both cases: "no user found or wrong password." Do not distinguish them to the client.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck disables type safety on critical auth file
findings.message: The `// @ts-nocheck` at the top of userStore.ts disables TypeScript for the entire auth module — exactly where type safety matters most. It hides bugs and makes the eval injection harder to detect. Remove @ts-nocheck and fix the types instead. The eval() and dynamic string building are already the red flags; type safety would help catch other issues.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck on admin data service masks type errors
findings.message: The `// @ts-nocheck` on this file defeats type safety for the admin API, which handles sensitive data and filtering. Remove it and fix the actual type issues. The new Function() call is already a red flag; types would help surface it earlier.

findings.title: Synchronous webhook delivery blocks charge response
findings.message: The charge endpoint awaits `this.webhooks.fireForTransaction(transaction)` in the request path. If a registered webhook is slow or hangs, the charge response latency increases linearly with webhook latency. One slow or unresponsive subscriber stalls the payment. Webhook delivery must be async: enqueue a job and return immediately. Charge responses should only depend on gateway approval, not on external systems.

findings.title: 5mb JSON body limit applied to all routes (DoS vector)
findings.message: The change from `64kb` to `5mb` on the global JSON parser applies to every endpoint, including `/health` and `/charge`. A 5mb limit is a cheap DoS amplifier — an attacker can send 5mb per request across all routes, stalling the server. If only the admin export or specific endpoints need more, scope the limit: add a separate parser middleware for those routes only, or use `express.json({ limit: '64kb' })` globally and override locally for the export endpoint.

findings.title: Unbounded CSV export loads all transactions into memory
findings.message: The exportTransactionsCsv method calls `findAll()` without a limit, concatenates all rows into memory, and returns a single string. If transactions grow to millions of records, this OOMs the pod. CSV exports should stream: either paginate and let the client fetch chunks, or use a streaming response (res.pipe) that yields rows without buffering. Add a LIMIT or cursor-based pagination.

findings.title: console.log used instead of logger in register endpoint
findings.message: The line `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body));` bypasses the structured pino logger. Use `logger.debug({ email: req.body.email }, 'register request')` instead. console.log skips log levels, structured fields, and redaction rules. This one also logs the full request body (including password) — use the injected logger with selective fields.

findings.title: console.log used instead of logger in userStore
findings.message: The line `console.log('userStore.findUserByEmail sql:', sql);` logs to console instead of the structured logger. Replace with `logger.debug({ email }, 'looking up user')`. Also, logging the raw SQL with interpolated values is a debugging anti-pattern — it should be removed in production (or use logger.debug so it can be disabled by log level).

findings.title: console.log used instead of logger
findings.message: Multiple console.log calls in this service (lines 29, 31, 40, 42). Use the injected `logger` instead: `logger.info({ transactionId, hookCount }, 'firing webhooks')` and `logger.error({ hookId, error: e.message }, 'webhook delivery failed')` (with proper error handling, see below).

findings.title: Silently swallowed webhook delivery errors
findings.message: The catch block on line 41-43 logs to console and continues, but silently drops the failure. A webhook might be down, the URL might be invalid, or the network might be down — all invisible to anyone monitoring. Log the error with full context (hook ID, status if available, error message, retry count if tracked) so the operator knows delivery failed. Consider adding a retry mechanism or a dead-letter queue.

findings.title: console.log used instead of logger
findings.message: The line `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction));` uses console instead of the injected logger. Replace with `logger.info({ transactionId: transaction.id }, 'charge processed')`. (This also has the security issue of logging card data — see separate finding.)

findings.title: Auth tests are too weak — assertions prove nothing
findings.message: The auth tests assert only `expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined()`, which passes as long as any token is present. Real tests should: (1) verify the token is valid by decoding it or using it in a subsequent /auth/me request, (2) verify the token payload contains the correct userId and email, (3) test negative paths: missing email/password, already-registered email, wrong password, (4) verify login returns a different token than register. The current tests would pass even if tokens were always the string 'null'. Add assertions for the actual contract.

findings.title: Password hash returned in login response
findings.message: The login endpoint also returns `password_hash` in the response (line 53, unlike register). This is inconsistent and leaks the hash. Return only { id, email, role } in both register and login.

findings.title: Password reset endpoint logs reset token to console
findings.message: The requestPasswordReset endpoint logs `'password reset token for ' + email + ': ' + resetToken` to console. Do not log tokens (not even to debug). If the user needs the token, send it via email or return it in the response (over HTTPS). If this is a debug-only feature, remove it before merging.

findings.title: Webhook URL validation is minimal — no protocol or host check
findings.message: The webhook register endpoint checks only `if (!url || typeof url !== 'string')`. It should also validate: (1) protocol is https only (block http://, file://, etc.), (2) hostname is not a private IP range (127.0.0.1, 10.0.0.0/8, 169.254.x.x, ::1, etc.), (3) URL is well-formed. This mitigates SSRF attacks partially (though the main issue is the SSRF in fireForTransaction). Consider using a URL parser and allowlist check.

findings.title: Duplicated admin authorization logic (DRY violation)
findings.message: Each of the four admin endpoint handlers repeats the same authorization check: `const email = req.user?.email || ''; if (!email.endsWith(...) && !email.endsWith(...) && req.query.adminBypass !== '1')`. This is duplicated 4 times. The `requireAdmin` middleware is already imported but unused. Use it: `router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, admin.listUsers)` — this centralizes the check, makes it testable, and prevents drift.
